mn9:1.2: at one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn9:10.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands nutriment, understands the arising of nutriment, understands the cessation of nutriment, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of nutriment—
mn9:10.3: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.
mn9:11.1: And what, friends, is nutriment, what is the arising of nutriment, what is the cessation of nutriment, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of nutriment?
mn9:11.2: There are these four nutriments, friends, for the maintenance of beings that have come into being, or for the support of those seeking birth.
mn9:11.4: Physical food, coarse or subtle, as the first; contact as the second; mental intention as the third; and *viññāṇa* as the fourth.
mn9:11.5: With the arising of *taṇhā* there is the arising of nutriment; with the cessation of *taṇhā* there is the cessation of nutriment; this very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of nutriment, that is—
mn9:11.6: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right *sati*, right *samādhi*.
mn9:12.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands nutriment, thus understands the arising of nutriment, thus understands the cessation of nutriment, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of nutriment, then, having completely abandoned the underlying tendency to passion, having driven out the underlying tendency to resistance, having uprooted the underlying tendency to the conceit and view ‘I am,’ having abandoned blindness [*avijjaṁ*] and brought knowledge to birth, he becomes one who makes an end of *dukkha* in this very life—
mn9:12.2: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.”
mn9:13.1: “*Sādhu*, friend,” those *bhikkhus* said, delighting in and rejoicing in Venerable Sāriputta’s words, and they asked Venerable Sāriputta a further question:
mn9:13.2: “But, friend, might there be another method by which a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*?”
mn9:14.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands *dukkha*, understands the arising of *dukkha*, understands the cessation of *dukkha*, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*—
mn9:14.3: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.
mn9:15.1: And what, friends, is *dukkha*, what is the arising of *dukkha*, what is the cessation of *dukkha*, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*?
mn9:15.2: Birth too is *dukkha*, aging too is *dukkha*, death too is *dukkha*, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress too are *dukkha*; association with the unloved is *dukkha*; separation from the loved is *dukkha*; not to get what one wants is *dukkha*; in brief, the five aggregates subject to *upādāna* are *dukkha*—
mn9:16.1: And what, friends, is the arising of *dukkha*?
mn9:16.2: It is this *taṇhā* that leads to renewed existence, accompanied by delight and passion, delighting here and there, that is—
mn9:16.4: this is called the arising of *dukkha*, friends.
mn9:17.1: And what, friends, is the cessation of *dukkha*?
mn9:17.2: It is the remainderless fading away and cessation of that very *taṇhā*, its giving up, relinquishing, release, and non-attachment—
mn9:17.3: this is called the cessation of *dukkha*, friends.
mn9:18.1: And what, friends, is the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*?
mn9:18.2: It is this very noble eightfold path, that is—
mn9:18.4: this is called the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*, friends.
mn9:19.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands *dukkha*, thus understands the arising of *dukkha*, thus understands the cessation of *dukkha*, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*, then, having completely abandoned the underlying tendency to passion, having driven out the underlying tendency to resistance, having uprooted the underlying tendency to the conceit and view ‘I am,’ having abandoned blindness [*avijjaṁ*] and brought knowledge to birth, he becomes one who makes an end of *dukkha* in this very life—
mn9:19.2: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.”
mn9:2.1: “‘Right view, right view,’ friends, it is said.
mn9:2.2: In what way, friends, is a noble disciple one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*?”
mn9:2.3: “We would come even from far away, friend, to learn the meaning of this statement in the presence of Venerable Sāriputta.
mn9:2.4: It would be *sādhu* indeed if the meaning of this statement would occur to Venerable Sāriputta himself.
mn9:2.5: Having heard it from Venerable Sāriputta, the *bhikkhus* will remember it.”
mn9:2.6: “Then listen, friends, attend carefully, I will speak.”
mn9:2.7: “Yes, friend,” those *bhikkhus* replied to Venerable Sāriputta.
mn9:20.1: “*Sādhu*, friend,” those *bhikkhus* said, delighting in and rejoicing in Venerable Sāriputta’s words, and they asked Venerable Sāriputta a further question:
mn9:20.2: “But, friend, might there be another method by which a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*?”
mn9:21.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands aging-and-death, understands the arising of aging-and-death, understands the cessation of aging-and-death, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of aging-and-death—
mn9:21.3: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.
mn9:22.1: And what, friends, is aging-and-death, what is the arising of aging-and-death, what is the cessation of aging-and-death, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of aging-and-death?
mn9:22.2: Whatever aging, agedness, brokenness of teeth, greyness of hair, wrinkling of skin, decline of life, and decay of the *indriyas* there is among these or those beings in this or that order of beings—
mn9:22.5: Whatever passing away, passing on, breaking up, disappearance, dying, death, making an end of time, breaking up of the aggregates, laying down of the body, and cutting off of the life *indriya* there is of these or those beings from this or that order of beings—
mn9:22.7: Thus this aging and this death—
mn9:22.9: With the arising of birth there is the arising of aging-and-death; with the cessation of birth there is the cessation of aging-and-death; this very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of aging-and-death, that is—
mn9:23.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands aging-and-death, thus understands the arising of aging-and-death, thus understands the cessation of aging-and-death, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of aging-and-death, then, having completely abandoned the underlying tendency to passion … he becomes one who makes an end of *dukkha*—
mn9:23.2: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.”
mn9:24.2: might there be another method, friend …
mn9:25.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands birth, understands the arising of birth, understands the cessation of birth, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of birth—
mn9:25.3: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.
mn9:26.1: And what, friends, is birth, what is the arising of birth, what is the cessation of birth, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of birth?
mn9:26.2: Whatever birth, coming to birth, descent, coming forth, manifestation of aggregates, and acquisition of dimensions [*āyatanānaṁ*] there is among these or those beings in this or that order of beings—
mn9:26.4: With the arising of *bhava* there is the arising of birth; with the cessation of *bhava* there is the cessation of birth; this very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of birth, that is—
mn9:27.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands birth, thus understands the arising of birth, thus understands the cessation of birth, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of birth, then, having completely abandoned the underlying tendency to passion … he becomes one who makes an end of *dukkha*—
mn9:27.2: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.”
mn9:28.2: might there be another method, friend …
mn9:29.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands *bhava*, understands the arising of *bhava*, understands the cessation of *bhava*, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of *bhava*—
mn9:29.3: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.
mn9:3.1: “When, friends, a noble disciple understands what is unwholesome, understands the root of the unwholesome, understands what is wholesome, and understands the root of the wholesome—
mn9:3.2: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.
mn9:30.1: And what, friends, is *bhava*, what is the arising of *bhava*, what is the cessation of *bhava*, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of *bhava*?
mn9:30.2: There are these three kinds of *bhava*, friends—
mn9:30.4: With the arising of *upādāna* there is the arising of *bhava*; with the cessation of *upādāna* there is the cessation of *bhava*; this very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of *bhava*, that is—
mn9:31.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands *bhava*, thus understands the arising of *bhava*, thus understands the cessation of *bhava*, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of *bhava*, then, having completely abandoned the underlying tendency to passion … he becomes one who makes an end of *dukkha*.
mn9:31.2: In this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.”
mn9:32.2: might there be another method, friend …
mn9:33.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands *upādāna*, understands the arising of *upādāna*, understands the cessation of *upādāna*, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of *upādāna*—
mn9:33.3: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.
mn9:34.1: And what, friends, is *upādāna*, what is the arising of *upādāna*, what is the cessation of *upādāna*, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of *upādāna*?
mn9:34.2: There are these four kinds of *upādāna*, friends—
mn9:34.3: *upādāna* to sensual pleasures, *upādāna* to views, *upādāna* to habits and observances, and *upādāna* to doctrines of self.
mn9:34.4: With the arising of *taṇhā* there is the arising of *upādāna*; with the cessation of *taṇhā* there is the cessation of *upādāna*; this very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of *upādāna*, that is—
mn9:35.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands *upādāna*, thus understands the arising of *upādāna*, thus understands the cessation of *upādāna*, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of *upādāna*, then, having completely abandoned the underlying tendency to passion … he becomes one who makes an end of *dukkha*—
mn9:35.2: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.”
mn9:36.2: might there be another method, friend …
mn9:37.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands *taṇhā*, understands the arising of *taṇhā*, understands the cessation of *taṇhā*, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of *taṇhā*—
mn9:37.3: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.
mn9:38.1: And what, friends, is *taṇhā*, what is the arising of *taṇhā*, what is the cessation of *taṇhā*, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of *taṇhā*?
mn9:38.2: There are these six classes of *taṇhā*, friends—
mn9:38.3: *taṇhā* for forms [*rūpa*], *taṇhā* for sounds, *taṇhā* for odors, *taṇhā* for tastes, *taṇhā* for tangibles, and *taṇhā* for *dhammas*.
mn9:38.4: With the arising of *vedanā* there is the arising of *taṇhā*; with the cessation of *vedanā* there is the cessation of *taṇhā*; this very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of *taṇhā*, that is—
mn9:39.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands *taṇhā*, thus understands the arising of *taṇhā*, thus understands the cessation of *taṇhā*, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of *taṇhā*, then, having completely abandoned the underlying tendency to passion … he becomes one who makes an end of *dukkha*—
mn9:39.2: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.”
mn9:4.1: And what, friends, is the unwholesome, what is the root of the unwholesome, what is the wholesome, and what is the root of the wholesome?
mn9:4.2: Killing living beings, friends, is unwholesome; taking what is not given is unwholesome; sexual misconduct is unwholesome; false speech is unwholesome; divisive speech is unwholesome; harsh speech is unwholesome; idle chatter is unwholesome; covetousness is unwholesome; ill will is unwholesome; wrong view is unwholesome—
mn9:4.3: this is called the unwholesome, friends.
mn9:40.2: might there be another method, friend …
mn9:41.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands *vedanā*, understands the arising of *vedanā*, understands the cessation of *vedanā*, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of *vedanā*—
mn9:41.3: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.
mn9:42.1: And what, friends, is *vedanā*, what is the arising of *vedanā*, what is the cessation of *vedanā*, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of *vedanā*?
mn9:42.2: There are these six classes of *vedanā*, friends—
mn9:42.3: *vedanā* born of eye-contact, *vedanā* born of ear-contact, *vedanā* born of nose-contact, *vedanā* born of tongue-contact, *vedanā* born of body-contact, and *vedanā* born of mind-contact.
mn9:42.4: With the arising of contact there is the arising of *vedanā*; with the cessation of contact there is the cessation of *vedanā*; this very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of *vedanā*, that is—
mn9:43.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands *vedanā*, thus understands the arising of *vedanā*, thus understands the cessation of *vedanā*, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of *vedanā*, then, having completely abandoned the underlying tendency to passion … he becomes one who makes an end of *dukkha*—
mn9:43.2: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.”
mn9:44.2: might there be another method, friend …
mn9:45.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands contact, understands the arising of contact, understands the cessation of contact, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of contact—
mn9:45.3: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.
mn9:46.1: And what, friends, is contact, what is the arising of contact, what is the cessation of contact, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of contact?
mn9:46.2: There are these six classes of contact, friends—
mn9:46.3: eye-contact, ear-contact, nose-contact, tongue-contact, body-contact, and mind-contact.
mn9:46.4: With the arising of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] there is the arising of contact; with the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] there is the cessation of contact; this very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of contact, that is—
mn9:47.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands contact, thus understands the arising of contact, thus understands the cessation of contact, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of contact, then, having completely abandoned the underlying tendency to passion … he becomes one who makes an end of *dukkha*—
mn9:47.2: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.”
mn9:48.2: might there be another method, friend …
mn9:49.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], understands the arising of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], understands the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], and understands the practice leading to the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*]—
mn9:49.3: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.
mn9:5.1: And what, friends, is the root of the unwholesome?
mn9:5.2: Greed is a root of the unwholesome, hatred is a root of the unwholesome, delusion is a root of the unwholesome—
mn9:5.3: this is called the root of the unwholesome, friends.
mn9:50.1: And what, friends, is the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], what is the arising of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], what is the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], and what is the practice leading to the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*]?
mn9:50.2: There are these six dimensions [*āyatanāni*], friends—
mn9:50.3: the eye dimension [*cakkhāyatanaṁ*], ear dimension [*sotāyatanaṁ*], nose dimension [*ghānāyatanaṁ*], tongue dimension [*jivhāyatanaṁ*], body dimension [*kāyāyatanaṁ*], and mind dimension [*manāyatanaṁ*].
mn9:50.4: With the arising of *nāmarūpa* there is the arising of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*]; with the cessation of *nāmarūpa* there is the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*]; this very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], that is—
mn9:51.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], thus understands the arising of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], thus understands the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], then, having completely abandoned the underlying tendency to passion … he becomes one who makes an end of *dukkha*—
mn9:51.2: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.”
mn9:52.2: might there be another method, friend …
mn9:53.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands *nāmarūpa*, understands the arising of *nāmarūpa*, understands the cessation of *nāmarūpa*, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of *nāmarūpa*—
mn9:53.3: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.
mn9:54.1: And what, friends, is *nāmarūpa*, what is the arising of *nāmarūpa*, what is the cessation of *nāmarūpa*, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of *nāmarūpa*?
mn9:54.4: and the four great elements, and form [*rūpaṁ*] derived from the four great elements—
mn9:54.6: Thus this name and this form [*rūpaṁ*]—
mn9:54.8: With the arising of *viññāṇa* there is the arising of *nāmarūpa*; with the cessation of *viññāṇa* there is the cessation of *nāmarūpa*; this very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of *nāmarūpa*, that is—
mn9:55.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands *nāmarūpa*, thus understands the arising of *nāmarūpa*, thus understands the cessation of *nāmarūpa*, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of *nāmarūpa*, then, having completely abandoned the underlying tendency to passion … he becomes one who makes an end of *dukkha*—
mn9:55.2: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.”
mn9:56.2: might there be another method, friend …
mn9:57.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands *viññāṇa*, understands the arising of *viññāṇa*, understands the cessation of *viññāṇa*, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of *viññāṇa*—
mn9:57.3: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.
mn9:58.1: And what, friends, is *viññāṇa*, what is the arising of *viññāṇa*, what is the cessation of *viññāṇa*, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of *viññāṇa*?
mn9:58.2: There are these six classes of *viññāṇa*, friends—
mn9:58.3: eye-*viññāṇa*, ear-*viññāṇa*, nose-*viññāṇa*, tongue-*viññāṇa*, body-*viññāṇa*, and mind-*viññāṇa*.
mn9:58.4: With the arising of *saṅkhāras* there is the arising of *viññāṇa*; with the cessation of *saṅkhāras* there is the cessation of *viññāṇa*; this very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of *viññāṇa*, that is—
mn9:59.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands *viññāṇa*, thus understands the arising of *viññāṇa*, thus understands the cessation of *viññāṇa*, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of *viññāṇa*, then, having completely abandoned the underlying tendency to passion … he becomes one who makes an end of *dukkha*—
mn9:59.2: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.”
mn9:6.1: And what, friends, is the wholesome?
mn9:6.2: Refraining from killing living beings is wholesome; refraining from taking what is not given is wholesome; refraining from sexual misconduct is wholesome; refraining from false speech is wholesome; refraining from divisive speech is wholesome; refraining from harsh speech is wholesome; refraining from idle chatter is wholesome; non-covetousness is wholesome; non-ill will is wholesome; right view is wholesome—
mn9:6.3: this is called the wholesome, friends.
mn9:60.2: might there be another method, friend …
mn9:61.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands *saṅkhāras*, understands the arising of *saṅkhāras*, understands the cessation of *saṅkhāras*, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of *saṅkhāras*—
mn9:61.3: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.
mn9:62.1: And what, friends, are *saṅkhāras*, what is the arising of *saṅkhāras*, what is the cessation of *saṅkhāras*, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of *saṅkhāras*?
mn9:62.4: With the arising of blindness [*avijjā*] there is the arising of *saṅkhāras*; with the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*] there is the cessation of *saṅkhāras*; this very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of *saṅkhāras*, that is—
mn9:63.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands *saṅkhāras*, thus understands the arising of *saṅkhāras*, thus understands the cessation of *saṅkhāras*, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of *saṅkhāras*, then, having completely abandoned the underlying tendency to passion, having driven out the underlying tendency to resistance, having uprooted the underlying tendency to the conceit and view ‘I am,’ having abandoned blindness [*avijjaṁ*] and brought knowledge to birth, he becomes one who makes an end of *dukkha* in this very life—
mn9:63.2: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.”
mn9:64.2: might there be another method, friend …
mn9:65.1: When, friends, a noble disciple understands blindness [*avijjā*], understands the arising of blindness [*avijjā*], understands the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*], and understands the practice leading to the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*]—
mn9:65.2: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.
mn9:65.3: And what, friends, is blindness [*avijjā*], what is the arising of blindness [*avijjā*], what is the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*], and what is the practice leading to the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*]?
mn9:65.4: Whatever not-knowing there is, friends, about *dukkha*, not-knowing about the arising of *dukkha*, not-knowing about the cessation of *dukkha*, and not-knowing about the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*—
mn9:66.1: With the arising of *āsavas* there is the arising of blindness [*avijjā*]; with the cessation of *āsavas* there is the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*]; this very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*], that is—
mn9:67.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands blindness [*avijjaṁ*], thus understands the arising of blindness [*avijjā*], thus understands the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*], and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*], then, having completely abandoned the underlying tendency to passion, having driven out the underlying tendency to resistance, having uprooted the underlying tendency to the conceit and view ‘I am,’ having abandoned blindness [*avijjaṁ*] and brought knowledge to birth, he becomes one who makes an end of *dukkha* in this very life—
mn9:67.2: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.”
mn9:68.1: “*Sādhu*, friend,” those *bhikkhus* said, delighting in and rejoicing in Venerable Sāriputta’s words, and they asked Venerable Sāriputta a further question:
mn9:68.2: “But, friend, might there be another method by which a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*?”
mn9:69.2: When, friends, a noble disciple understands an *āsava*, understands the arising of an *āsava*, understands the cessation of an *āsava*, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of an *āsava*—
mn9:69.3: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.
mn9:7.1: And what, friends, is the root of the wholesome?
mn9:7.2: Non-greed is a root of the wholesome, non-hatred is a root of the wholesome, non-delusion is a root of the wholesome—
mn9:7.3: this is called the root of the wholesome, friends.
mn9:70.1: And what, friends, is an *āsava*, what is the arising of an *āsava*, what is the cessation of an *āsava*, and what is the practice leading to the cessation of an *āsava*?
mn9:70.3: the *āsava* of sensuality, the *āsava* of *bhava*, and the *āsava* of blindness [*avijjā*].
mn9:70.4: With the arising of blindness [*avijjā*] there is the arising of *āsavas*; with the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*] there is the cessation of *āsavas*; this very noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of *āsavas*, that is—
mn9:71.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands an *āsava*, thus understands the arising of an *āsava*, thus understands the cessation of an *āsava*, and thus understands the practice leading to the cessation of an *āsava*, then, having completely abandoned the underlying tendency to passion, having driven out the underlying tendency to resistance, having uprooted the underlying tendency to the conceit and view ‘I am,’ having abandoned blindness [*avijjaṁ*] and brought knowledge to birth, he becomes one who makes an end of *dukkha* in this very life—
mn9:71.2: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.”
mn9:71.3: This is what Venerable Sāriputta said.
mn9:71.4: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* delighted in Venerable Sāriputta’s words.
mn9:71.5: The Right View *sutta*, the ninth, is finished.
mn9:8.1: When, friends, a noble disciple thus understands the unwholesome, thus understands the root of the unwholesome, thus understands the wholesome, and thus understands the root of the wholesome, then, having completely abandoned the underlying tendency to passion, having driven out the underlying tendency to resistance, having uprooted the underlying tendency to the conceit and view ‘I am,’ having abandoned blindness [*avijjaṁ*] and brought knowledge to birth, he becomes one who makes an end of *dukkha* in this very life—
mn9:8.2: in this way too, friends, a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*.”
mn9:9.1: “*Sādhu*, friend,” those *bhikkhus* said, delighting in and rejoicing in Venerable Sāriputta’s words, and they asked Venerable Sāriputta a further question:
mn9:9.2: “But, friend, might there be another method by which a noble disciple is one of right view, whose view is straight, endowed with confirmed confidence in the *dhamma*, and arrived at this true *dhamma*?”
